Environmental Plastic Waste Analysis

Author

Shravani Subramanian 

library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(glue)
library(purrr)
library(countrycode)
library(forstringr)
library(scales)
library(hexbin)

Context

The Environmental Plastic Waste Data comes from the “Break Free from Plastic” movement and covers information on plastic use across the world, organized by country, type of plastic, source of plastic, and year (2019 or 2020). The data comes from brand audits and cleanups conducted by volunteers. Some limitations of this dataset are that the countries will not have an equal sampling effort and the data depends on volunteer participation. Additionally, the amount of collected waste does not necessarily tell us how much plastic there is in general. Aside from those, the data is useful to help identify major corporate contributors to plastic pollution in our environment. It can also help inform environmental policy workers and provide corporate accountability.

Data Cleaning

It seems as though initially the 2019 and 2020 datasets were imported separately from directories. Only the csv files were chosen from those respective directories. We then read the two CSV files with specified column types to prevent any confusion. The code then combines multiple files into a single file for each year (one for 2019 and one for 2020) through map_dfr. Next, the code cleans column names for both files using the janitor::clean_names() command, and makes the names consistent across years. A year column is also added to the datasets.

Individual Changes:

2020:

  • The Grand_Total variable is converted from character to numeric.

2019:

  • The country names are extracted from file names & stored in a country column.

  • Combines duplicate columns pp_2 and pp as well as ps & ps_2.

  • Handles missing values using is.na and an ifelse() statement.

  • Drops ps_2 & pp_2

Lastly, the 2019 and 2020 datasets are merged using bind_rows()and exported to a csv file.

Research Questions

Using Current Data:

  1. Have certain plastic types (e.g. Polystyrene or High density polyethylene) become more or less common over time?

Using Supplemental Data:

  1. How does annual mean temperature affect total plastic count?

  2. Do countries with higher GDPs have more plastic counting events, and thus a higher total plastic count?

Checkpoint 3

Temperature Data Set (Additional Country-Level Data)

# Read in Temperature data set
temp <- read.csv("combined_temperature.csv")

temp <- temp |>
  filter(Year %in% (c(2019, 2020)),) |>
  rename(AnnualMeanTemp = Annual.Mean)

# Show first 10 rows of temp data set
head(temp, 10)
              Country Year AnnualMeanTemp X5.yr.smooth Code
1  Dominican Republic 2019          24.91        24.90  DOM
2  Dominican Republic 2020          24.91        24.81  DOM
3       United States 2019           9.73        10.12  USA
4       United States 2020          10.02        10.00  USA
5              Zambia 2019          22.40        22.21  ZMB
6              Zambia 2020          22.03        22.13  ZMB
7             Vanuatu 2019          24.37        24.54  VUT
8             Vanuatu 2020          24.73        24.61  VUT
9               Tonga 2019          25.18        25.20  TON
10              Tonga 2020          25.40        25.19  TON

The temperature data set includes data on the mean annual temperatures for all countries worldwide for the years 1901-2022, but we are particularly interested in the years 2019 and 2020. The 3-letter ISO code for each country is also included. We chose this data set because we wanted to explore how climate affects plastic degradation and consumption across different countries around the world.

GDP Data Set (Additional Country-Level Data)

get_gdp <- function(country, key = my_key) {
  safe_parse <- safely(
    function(country, key) {

      response <- GET(
        url = "https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/gdp",
        query = list(country = country),
        add_headers("X-Api-Key" = key)
      )

      data <- content(response, as = "text", encoding = "UTF-8")
      fromJSON(data)
    }
  )

  out <- safe_parse(country, key)

  # if there was an error, return empty data frame
  if (!is.null(out$error)) {
    return(data.frame())
  }

  # if result is empty, return empty data frame
  if (length(out$result) == 0) {
    return(data.frame())
  }

  # force result into data frame
  as.data.frame(out$result) |> filter(year %in% c(2019, 2020))
}

# Test Case
# can call function with 2-letter ISO code (get_gdp("CA")) or with full country name:
get_gdp("Canada")
gdp <- map_dfr(unique(temp$Code), get_gdp)
head(gdp, 10)
write_csv(x = gdp, file = "stat431-gdp.csv")

The original GDP data set includes historical, current, and predicted data on GDP for countries around the world for the years for 1980-2029. We are particularly interested in the years 2019 and 2020. The 3-letter ISO code for each country is also included. There are 5 variables measuring GDP in different ways. For example, the variable gdp_per_capita_nominal measures GDP by dividing the country’s total value of finished goods and services by its total population. gdp_nominal is the nominal GDP in billions of US dollars and gdp_growth expresses the GDP growth rate as a percentage.

gdp <- read_csv("stat431-gdp.csv")
Rows: 325 Columns: 8
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (1): country
dbl (7): year, gdp_growth, gdp_nominal, gdp_per_capita_nominal, gdp_ppp, gdp...

“Meta” Dataset: Combining Temperature, GDP, and Plastic Waste Data Sets

# Keep gdp_per_capita_nominal variable
gdp_per_capita <- gdp |>
  select(country, year, gdp_nominal, gdp_per_capita_nominal)

# Remove X5.yr.smooth variable from temperature data, join with gdp data set
combined_temp_gdp <- temp |>
  select(-X5.yr.smooth) |>
  left_join(gdp_per_capita, by = c("Code" = "country", "Year" = "year"))
plastics <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2021/2021-01-26/plastics.csv')
Rows: 13380 Columns: 14
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr  (2): country, parent_company
dbl (12): year, empty, hdpe, ldpe, o, pet, pp, ps, pvc, grand_total, num_eve...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
# Sum up plastic and grand total plastic counts for each country, for each year
plastics_sum <- plastics |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarize(
    across(empty:grand_total,\(x) sum(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
    num_events = first(num_events),
    volunteers = first(volunteers),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
head(plastics_sum, 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 13
   country     year empty  hdpe  ldpe     o   pet    pp    ps   pvc grand_total
   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl>
 1 Argentina   2019     0   430   110  1214  2752   562   232    36        5336
 2 Argentina   2020   128   132   141   212   144   263    62     4        1086
 3 Armenia     2020     0     0     0     0     8     0     0     0           8
 4 Australia   2019     0     0     0     0     6     0     0     0           6
 5 Australia   2020     4    33   222   898   141   361     3     6        1668
 6 Bangladesh  2019     0     0     0    32     0     0     0     0          32
 7 Bangladesh  2020     0   376     2  1559   299    89    58     0        2383
 8 Benin       2019     0     0     0     0  9976     0     0     0        9976
 9 Benin       2020     0     0     0     0   342     0     0     0         342
10 Bhutan      2019     0     0     0     0  7000     0     0     0        7000
# ℹ 2 more variables: num_events <dbl>, volunteers <dbl>
# Create country code variable for plastics data
plastics_sum <- plastics_sum |>
  filter(!(country == "EMPTY")) |>
  mutate(Code = countrycode(sourcevar = country, origin = "country.name", destination = "iso3c")) |>
  rename(Year = year, Country = country)

head(plastics_sum, 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 14
   Country     Year empty  hdpe  ldpe     o   pet    pp    ps   pvc grand_total
   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>       <dbl>
 1 Argentina   2019     0   430   110  1214  2752   562   232    36        5336
 2 Argentina   2020   128   132   141   212   144   263    62     4        1086
 3 Armenia     2020     0     0     0     0     8     0     0     0           8
 4 Australia   2019     0     0     0     0     6     0     0     0           6
 5 Australia   2020     4    33   222   898   141   361     3     6        1668
 6 Bangladesh  2019     0     0     0    32     0     0     0     0          32
 7 Bangladesh  2020     0   376     2  1559   299    89    58     0        2383
 8 Benin       2019     0     0     0     0  9976     0     0     0        9976
 9 Benin       2020     0     0     0     0   342     0     0     0         342
10 Bhutan      2019     0     0     0     0  7000     0     0     0        7000
# ℹ 3 more variables: num_events <dbl>, volunteers <dbl>, Code <chr>
# Combine plastics data with temp/GDP
meta_data <- inner_join(plastics_sum, combined_temp_gdp, by = c("Code", "Year")) |>
  select(-Country.y) |>
  rename(Country = Country.x)

How does annual mean temperature affect total plastic count?

# Grand Total Plastic Count by Annual Mean Temperature
ggplot(data = meta_data, aes(x = AnnualMeanTemp, y = grand_total, color = factor(Year))) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) + 
  labs(x = "Annual Mean Temperature (Celsius)",
       y = "Grand Total Plastic Count",
       title = "Grand Total Plastic Count by Annual Mean Temperature",
       color = "Year") + 
  scale_color_manual(values = c("2019" = "steelblue", "2020" = "lightcoral")) + 
  theme_classic()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Do countries with higher GDPs have more plastic counting events, and thus a higher total plastic count?

# Grand Total Plastic Count by Number of Plastic Counting Events
ggplot(data = meta_data, aes(x = num_events, y = grand_total, color = factor(Year))) +
  geom_point(aes(size = gdp_per_capita_nominal)) +
  labs(x = "Number of Plastic Counting Events",
       y = "Grand Total Plastic Count",
       title = "Grand Total Plastic Count by Number of Plastic Counting Events",
       color = "Year", 
       size = "Nominal GDP per Capital") + 
  scale_color_manual(values = c("2019" = "steelblue", "2020" = "lightcoral")) + 
  theme_classic()
Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).
write_csv(x=meta_data, file = "stat431-meta.csv")

Checkpoint 4

plastic_temp_gdp <- read_csv("stat431-meta.csv")
plastic_temp_gdp <- plastic_temp_gdp |>
  mutate(continent = countrycode(Country,
                                  origin = "country.name",
                                  destination = "continent"))

RQ #1: How does change in GDP relate to change in the total amount of plastic collected?

changed <- plastic_temp_gdp |>
  group_by(Country, continent, Year) |>
  summarise(grand_total = sum(grand_total, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Year, values_from = grand_total) |>
  rename(
    total_2019 = `2019`,
    total_2020 = `2020`
  ) |>
  mutate(change = total_2020 - total_2019) |>
  drop_na(change, continent)
continent_year <- plastic_temp_gdp |>
  group_by(continent, Year) |>
  summarise(grand_total = sum(grand_total, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(continent_year, aes(x = continent, y = grand_total, fill = factor(Year))) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  labs(
    title = "Grand Total by Continent (2019 vs 2020)",
    x = "Continent",
    y = "Grand Total",
    fill = "Year"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "lightgreen")) +
  theme_classic() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
ggplot(changed, aes(x = continent, y = change)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Change in Grand Total by Continent",
    subtitle = "Country-level change from 2019 to 2020",
    x = "Continent",
    y = "Change (2020 - 2019)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

We noticed an outlier in Africa that was that was heavily compressing the scale of the boxplot. We identified the outlier to be Nigeria.

ggplot(changed, aes(x = continent, y = change)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-30000, 30000))+
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Change in Grand Total by Continent",
    subtitle = "Country-level change from 2019 to 2020, excluding Nigeria",
    x = "Continent",
    y = "Change (2020 - 2019)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
continent_year <- plastic_temp_gdp |>
  group_by(continent, Year) |>
  summarise(gdp_nominal = sum(gdp_nominal, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(continent_year, aes(x = continent, y = gdp_nominal, fill = factor(Year))) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  labs(
    title = "Nominal GDP by Continent (2019 vs 2020)",
    x = "Continent",
    y = "Nominal GDP",
    fill = "Year"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "lightgreen")) +
  theme_classic() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
# Calculating Percent Change in GDP from 2019 to 2020
gdp_changed <- plastic_temp_gdp %>%
  arrange(Country, Year) %>%
  group_by(Country) %>%
  mutate(gdp_pct_change = (gdp_nominal / lag(gdp_nominal) - 1) * 100)
ggplot(gdp_changed, aes(x = continent, y = gdp_pct_change)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(y = "GDP % Change (2019-2020)", x = "Continent") + 
  theme_classic() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

RQ #2: Is there a relationship between a country’s mean annual temperature and the total amount of plastic collected?

ggplot(data = plastic_temp_gdp) +
  geom_hex(mapping = aes(x = AnnualMeanTemp, y = grand_total)) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "lightblue", high = "royalblue4") +
  geom_smooth(aes(x = AnnualMeanTemp, y = grand_total), 
              method = "lm", se = FALSE, 
              color = "black", linewidth = 0.5, lty = 2) +
  labs(x = "Mean Annual Temperature (C°)", 
       y = "Grand Total Plastic Collected", 
       fill = "Number of Countries",
       title = "Number of Countries Observed", 
       subtitle = "For Mean Annual Temperature, Grand Total Plastic Collected Combination (2019 - 2020)") +
  theme_classic() + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")) 
# Fitting a linear model
plastic.fit <- lm(grand_total~AnnualMeanTemp, data = plastic_temp_gdp)
summary(plastic.fit)

Call:
lm(formula = grand_total ~ AnnualMeanTemp, data = plastic_temp_gdp)
